topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the locker room seemed to hum with an aggressive, buzzing energy, amplifying the silence that followed Coach Miller’s announcement. Liam stood by his duffel bag, his knuckles white where he gripped the worn leather. Every muscle in his jaw felt tight, a knot of sudden, sharp tension. He stared at the scuffed linoleum floor, watching a stray piece of grit roll slowly toward the drain. When the coach finally finished speaking, the words—final, clipped, and utterly devoid of warmth—felt like physical blows. Liam didn't move for a long time. He slowly zipped his bag shut, the sound unnervingly loud in the sudden quiet. His breathing hitched, shallow and rapid, as he forced his gaze toward the exit, the desire to bolt overriding the instinct to remain.

[story 2]

"This isn't fair." The words escaped Maya’s lips, thin and reedy, sounding foreign even to her own ears. She clenched her fists so tightly that her nails dug crescent moons into her palms, the pain a welcome distraction from the hollow ache spreading through her chest. She kept running the scenario over and over in her mind: the training sessions, the shared victories, the trust they had built. Now, all that felt like a fragile illusion shattered. When Coach Evans pointed toward the exit, Maya didn't look at him. She just turned, her shoulders slumping forward as if gravity had suddenly doubled its pull. A hot flush crept up her neck, and she could feel the prickle behind her eyes, the desperate urge to rewind the last twenty minutes, to somehow undo the sting of that dismissal.

[story 3]

He walked the perimeter of the field three times, his boots crunching rhythmically on the dry turf, a mechanical motion designed to keep the churning inside from spilling out. Every glance he caught from his teammates was a sharp, unwelcome intrusion. He felt a strange numbness creeping into his extremities, a coldness that seemed to seep into his bones. When the coach’s decision was finalized, there was no outburst, no dramatic collapse. Instead, he simply stopped walking, his head dipping low. He let out a slow, almost silent exhale, the air tasting metallic in his mouth. He didn't look back at the team; he just focused on the distant line of trees, trying to anchor himself against the sudden, terrifying feeling of being utterly adrift.
